content: Just as the linguistic sense of reflexion taught in high school language learning requires learners to look back on their own expression to connect how their words reflect their intended meaning, LLM self-reflection mechanisms can borrow this educational framing to ground abstract self-correction in the core dual meaning of reflexion: both reflecting on errors and reflecting internal intent to align external outputs. We can hypothesize that fine-tuning LLMs to explicitly answer "what does this output reflect about my original reasoning goal" before self-correction will improve output alignment more than current inconsistency-based self-reflection approaches.
